reasoning: The Federal Reserve's liquidity and credit facilities (WLCFLL) exhibit a strong seasonal spike at year-end, which rapidly unwinds in January. Data pulled from FRED (as of Jan 18, 2026) shows this unwinding is aggressively underway: from $9,740M on Dec 31 to $7,287M on Jan 7, to $5,431M on Jan 14 (the baseline value for these resolutions). Early 2025 showed baseline borrowing hovering between $3,800M and $5,300M from Jan-May before trending higher in the second half of the year. Due to the very strong negative momentum (-$1,856M in the last week), it is likely that the value will continue to decrease for the Jan 21 observation (resolving Jan 25) as it completes the wash-out and settles near or below the $5,000M mark, making an increase less likely. By mid-February, the baseline will have stabilized, making a rebound or random spike slightly more possible due to the longer horizon, pushing the probability closer to 0.5.
